result:
I. The room that is not a room  
I keep returning, in memory, to a place that never existed in one piece. It is a room assembled from scraps of every bedroom I have ever slept in: the cracked ceiling of a student flat in Glasgow, the lavender walls of my grandmother’s house, the cold terrazzo floor of a hostel in Ljubljana, the single bare bulb that swung over my bed during the three months I lived above a butcher’s shop. The door is always ajar, although I never see the corridor outside. There is a window, but it opens onto nothing more specific than “afternoon.” Light pools on the floorboards like warm milk; dust motes revolve in it like slow planets. Sometimes the room contains a desk, sometimes only a chair with one leg shorter than the others. On the wall hangs a mirror that refuses to reflect me unless I meet its gaze with the exact disappointment it expects.  

I think of this room as the waiting area of my mind. Important parcels arrive there—regret, anticipation, a phrase whose music I have not yet earned—and are placed on the floor like unclaimed luggage. If I sit long enough, the parcels open themselves. A smell escapes: rain on hot asphalt, the metallic breath of the Underground, the sweet rot of pears in a bowl. Each odor is a ticket to somewhere else. I never know whether I am travelling away from or toward myself.  

II. On the difficulty of beginning  
Every act of writing starts before the first word, in the small, cowardly negotiations with the blank page. I promise myself I will write “later,” when I am better rested, when I have read one more book, when the moon is in a more inspirational quadrant. The page listens, patient and pitiless. It knows that later is a suburb of never. Still, I circle the airport of intention, burning fuel.  

There is a trick I play: I tell myself I am not really beginning, only tidying the runway. I open a notebook and copy out someone else’s sentence—say, Virginia Woolf’s “For now she need not think about anybody. She could be herself, by herself.” The ink of her words is a raft. I climb on, and suddenly my own sentences feel permitted to crawl out of the drowned hold where they have been waiting.  

III. Inventory of a voice  
What does my voice sound like when no one is meant to hear it? It is higher than I expect, slightly asthmatic, as if it still lives in the throat of the boy who was laughed at for mispronouncing “isthmus.” It carries the residue of every place that tried to teach it how to behave: the flat vowels of East Anglia, the rising terminals borrowed from Australian soap operas, the rolled “r” I practiced in front of the mirror after my first trip to Barcelona, hoping to smuggle exoticism into my ordinary blood.  

The voice is greedy. It collects accents the way magpies hoard foil. It comes home from trips abroad with unfamiliar cadences stuck to its shoes. In Cork I caught myself lilt; in Cairo I acquired a habit of starting stories with “Listen!” as if the world were hard of hearing. Each new music is a coat I try on, reluctant to leave the shop naked.  

But underneath the coats there is a body that remembers every humiliation. The voice that read aloud in class and heard someone snigger, “Who does he think he is?” The voice that left a message on a lover’s machine and never got a reply. Those wounds are tuning forks: strike them and the whole biography vibrates.  

IV. The archive of almost  
I own a box labeled “Almost.” Inside are rejected manuscripts, half-finished love letters, a map of a city I never visited because the war started the week I planned to go. There is a Polaroid of a dog I almost adopted; his name, scrawled on the white margin, is “Archie,” though I would have renamed him “Nabokov” had the shelter not called to say he’d been claimed an hour earlier.  

The artifacts hum with a dim electricity. They are evidence of alternative lives, each one a door I walked up to, knocked, then retreated. Sometimes, late at night, I open the box and try to knit the scraps into a single garment. The result is always a Frankenstein coat: sleeves of different lengths, pockets that open onto sealed seams. Still, I wear it around the apartment, checking for fit, dreaming of alterations.  

V. A brief history of my hands  
Age zero: hands are aliens, floating in front of the face, fascinating and ungovernable.  
Age four: they learn to hold a crayon vertically, like a dagger, and stab color into paper.  
Age seven: they are punished for flipping the bird to a teacher who confiscated the bird’s nest I brought for show-and-tell.  
Age twelve: they discover the guitar, grow calluses that are secret braille.  
Age fifteen: they tremble the first time they unhook a bra, fumbling like tourists with an unfamiliar seat belt.  
Age twenty: they type a suicide note, then delete it letter by letter, as if erasing the future could rewind the past.  
Age thirty: they learn to make bread, enjoy the obedience of dough.  
Age forty: they press against a lover’s sleeping back at 3 a.m., counting the small secret ribs, trying to memorize mortality.  

Now: they hover above the keyboard, middle-aged diplomats negotiating between what the heart howls and what the mouth can bear to say.  

VI. The year of the comet and other fragile calendars  
1997 was the year Hale-Bopp hung in the sky like a lingering chord. I was nineteen, living in a house where the heating had broken, so we kept the oven open for warmth. Friends came over with cheap wine and questions we mistook for original. We argued about whether the comet was a sign or merely a rock, about whether meaning was something the universe delivered or something we stitched into it like smuggled letters into a prison mattress.  

That winter I fell in love with a woman who painted her nails with White-Out correction fluid. She smelled of turpentine and oranges. We lay on the roof, backs against frosted tiles, watching the comet’s tail dissolve into the general dark. She said, “Everything that’s ever mattered to anyone is still traveling outward.” I thought she was being profound; later I realized she was quoting a song lyric. By spring the comet was gone and so was she, moved to Montreal with someone who owned actual furniture.  

Still, I date my private era from that passage. Not AD and BC, but Before Comet and After. The comet became the origin myth of every subsequent disappointment: love burns bright, departs, leaves you colder.  

VII. On the proliferation of small plastics  
I read that by 2050 there will be more plastic in the ocean than fish. This statistic lodges in me like a splinter. I close the laptop, walk to the shore of the city—an estuary where shopping trolleys stand in shallow water like the skeletons of ritual sacrifices. I pick up a bottle cap, a tampon applicator, a toy dinosaur missing its head. Each object once belonged to someone’s moment of convenience. A straw stirred a cocktail on a first date; a lighter flared at a funeral wake. Now they are evidence in an autopsy none of us agreed to perform.  

I carry the plastic home, wash it in the sink, arrange it on the windowsill. My intention is to bear witness, but within days the heap becomes domesticated. I stop seeing it; it joins the clutter of chargers and loose change. Guilt, too, plasticizes: flexible, colorful, endlessly reshapable.  

VIII. The neighbor who plays piano at 2 a.m.  
Every night, the same nocturne seeps through the wall. I have never seen the neighbor, though I’ve imagined her thoroughly: a retired accompanist with arthritic fingers, a heartbroken insomniac, a teenage prodigy forbidden to practice during daylight hours. The music is slightly out of tempo, as if the pianist is translating dreams into sound and the lag is the distance between languages.  

I lie in bed, jealous of her certainty. She knows what to do with longing: she lets it spill over keys. My own longing sits in me like unread mail. Some nights I rap on the wall, a feeble Morse: I am here. The playing pauses, resumes, unaware that it has been addressed.  

IX. A short treatise on holes  
There is the hole left by the pulled tooth, a cave your tongue keeps returning to, astonished by absence.  
The hole in the ozone layer, which we learned about in school alongside the hole in the Titanic, both catastrophes rendered in pastel textbooks that smelled of wet paper and puberty.  
The hole in a sock, through which the second toe pokes like a shy actor.  
The hole in the plot of every story I write, large enough to swallow motive.  
The hole at the center of the galaxy, sagging under the weight of its own invitation.  
The hole a person leaves when they exit a room faster than their explanation.  

We survive by filling holes with whatever is at hand: cotton, light, rumor, popcorn, other people. The tragedy is that the filler often takes the shape of the missing thing, so we walk around carrying molds of absence, proud of how closely they fit.  

X. The museum of obsolete smells  
Imagine a building whose galleries are organized not by era or geography but by scent. Corridor 1: Mimeograph ink, vinyl car seats, the sugary breath of cassette tapes unraveling. Corridor 2: The iron whiff of VHS tape dragged across magnetic heads, the eggy promise of polaroid developer, the stale exhalation of floppy disks. Corridor 3: Your grandmother’s handbag—tobacco, spearmint, the ghost of a lavender sachet.  

You buy a ticket, are handed a paper mask shaped like the face of your nine-year-old self. The mask filters nothing; it is only a reminder that nostalgia is a respirator through which the past becomes breathable, if not exactly healthy.  

At the exit there is a suggestion box. You write: “Please add the smell of my father’s workshop, which I can’t describe except to say it was the opposite of loneliness.” You drop the slip in the slot, knowing it will never be curated; the chemistry of lost love is too volatile to bottle.  

XI. Instructions for time travel without a machine  
1. Eat the brand of yogurt discontinued in 1998.  
2. Sit in a car parked under chestnut trees after rain; wait for the engine to cool until the hood ticks like a dying campfire.  
3. Hum the jingle of a local commercial that aired only once, at 6:15 on a Tuesday.  
4. Open the album to the photograph where someone’s thumb partially obscures the lens; stare until the thumb becomes a bridge you can walk across.  
5. Address an envelope to your former address, enclose a blank sheet, lick the stamp that tastes like childhood glue. Do not mail it; place it in a drawer.  
6. At 3 a.m., whisper the name of the street where you lost your first tooth. Whisper it backwards.  
7. Wait for the room to answer.  

XII. A conversation with my future corpse  
Me: Is it cold in there?  
Corpse: It is the exact temperature of every rejected embrace.  
Me: Did it hurt?  
Corpse: Only the living parts.  
Me: What should I do with the days that remain?  
Corpse: Touch fewer surfaces, more edges.  
Me: Will I see you again?  
Corpse: I am the last sentence you will write; every word before that is a postponement.  
Me: Can I at least finish this essay?  
Corpse: (shrugs) Essays finish themselves. The question is whether you will stay to watch the credits.  

XIII. The democracy of insomnia  
At 4:12 a.m., we are all citizens of the same beleaguered republic. The finance broker in his glass tower and the cleaner vacuuming the brokerage floor vote with their open eyes. The new mother listens to her baby’s breath, the widower listens to the absence that roars like tinnitus. The teenager scrolls until his thumb cramps, the novelist googles “symptoms of inoperable doubt.” No passport is required; only the inability to surrender to the gravity of tomorrow.  

I log on to a forum where insomniacs post photographs of what they see outside their windows. A streetlamp in Lagos, a snowed-under Renault in Kiev, a cat on a windowsill in São Paulo—same cat my grandmother claimed could smell death arriving two days early. We annotate each other’s skies. Someone writes beneath my upload of a blinking airplane: “We are all flight paths crossing without radar.” I reply: “May we land gently, eventually.”  

XIV. The paradox of the unfinished symphony  
Schubert’s Eighth is missing two movements. Musicologists argue whether he abandoned it or simply found the silence more eloquent. I think of all the things I have left half-built: a marriage that ended at the altar of maybe, a novel whose protagonist got tired of waiting and walked off the page. We treat incompleteness as failure, but what if it is the most honest signature we can leave? The universe itself is expanding, a work-in-progress that refuses deadline.  

I decide to write a manifesto for the perpetually unfinished. Rule one: Honor the draft. Rule two: When you reach the edge, plant flowers that thrive on cliffs. Rule three: Measure life not by what you finish but by what you are brave enough to begin knowing you cannot perfect. The manifesto reaches 412 words and stops, appropriately, mid-sentence.  

XV. The reconciliation of fragments  
I began by describing a room that is not a room, and I will end with a garden that is not a garden. It is a rectangle of weeds behind the apartment block, ignored by the landscaper, cultivated by neglect. Dandelions, nettles, a volunteer tomato plant that shouldn’t thrive but does. I go there at dusk when the sky looks like a deleted paragraph restored by an undo button.  

I squat, feel the chill through my jeans, and speak aloud the names of everyone I have failed to love well. The list is long; the weeds do not judge. Instead they offer modest miracles: a ladybug fastening its shell like a helmet, the tomato plant scenting the air with green optimism. I understand that the garden is the room’s antonym: where the room hoards, the garden relinquishes. Seeds abandon themselves to wind; weeds surrender their territory to frost.  

I tear out a page from the notebook I carry, tear the page into smaller pages, then into confetti. I bury the pieces among the roots. Tomorrow they will be unreadable, yesterday’s arrogance mulched into humus. Something else will grow here—maybe a phrase, maybe only the forgiveness of soil.  

I stand. The city’s lights flicker on, a constellation that refuses mythology. I think: perhaps the 2,500th word is not a milestone but an exit. I count the steps away from this paragraph and into whatever comes next.  

XVI. The unwritten novel  
There is a novel I will never write. Its protagonist is a woman who collects last words the way lepidopterists pin wings. She lives in a city built on top of another city; when the subway brakes, you can hear the dead one whisper. Every chapter begins with an epitaph she has stolen from a stranger’s deathbed. The plot refuses to cohere; it is a flock of birds that will not stay in formation. Critics would call it “fragmentary,” readers would call it “confusing,” my mother would call it “depressing.” So I keep it folded in a drawer, a paper kite that will never confront wind.  

Sometimes, late at night, I take the kite out and hold it up to the lamp. The light shines through the gaps where sentences should be. I pretend those gaps are windows, and that someone inside is looking back at me, equally afraid to finish what we started.  

XVII. Instructions for disappearing  
1. Begin by removing your name from the envelopes of bills. Let the electricity company guess who you were.  
2. Stop answering to your reflection. Nod politely in mirrors, then walk away mid-sentence.  
3. Plant something invasive—bamboo, mint, regret—so the garden argues your case after you’re gone.  
4. Leave the radio on, tuned to a station that plays only field recordings of airports.  
5. Forget your birthday on purpose. Feel the day pass like a stranger’s coat brushing your arm.  
6. Translate your diary into a language no one speaks, then lose the dictionary.  
7. Practice the smile that closes doors without sound.  
8. Finally, walk into the fog at the edge of town carrying nothing but the smell of rain on pavement. Do not look back; the town is learning to manage without your outlines.  

XVIII. The paradox of the second person  
I keep slipping into “you,” as if addressing someone outside the skin, when really the pronoun is a hand reaching back inside the ribcage. You, reader, are a conspiracy I invent to feel less alone. Yet the more I insist on you, the more I erase the border between us. Your pupils dilate; mine echo. Your heart makes a fist; mine rehearses the same shadow boxing. I write “you” and suddenly the room that is not a room contains two chairs, two cups of coffee cooling at different rates. The mirror finally consents to reflect, but the face is hybrid, half yours, half mine, like a photograph double-exposed by accident and declared art.  

XI. Recipe for something like happiness  
Take three ripe avocados, the kind that sigh when pressed.  
Add the juice of one lime that rolled under the fridge and gathered dust—its scars are flavor.  
Mince one small onion so fine that it dissolves into rumor.  
Salt until the mixture tastes like the first time you swore in front of your mother and she laughed instead of scolded.  
Fold in chopped cilantro, but only if your genes don’t make it taste like dish soap.  
Serve on toast burnt exactly to the color of your favorite childhood teddy bear after the dryer incident.  
Eat barefoot, standing at the kitchen counter, while the neighbor’s nocturne slips through the wall.  
Lick the bowl shamelessly; the bowl remembers every hunger you pretended not to have.  

XII. The paradox of distance  
The farther away someone is, the more permission we grant them to be perfect. A continent, an ocean, a silence of three unreturned messages—each mile polishes the statue we carve of the absent beloved. Up close, pores appear, contradictions, the way they chew too loudly. I wonder if God invented distance so we could practice forgiveness in gradients.  

XIII. Letter to my future ghost  
Dear Ghost of Me Who Has Finally Learned How to Leave,  
By the time you read this, the room that is not a room will have renovated itself again. Maybe the bulb has been replaced by something energy-efficient and smug. Maybe the chair with the short leg has been burned for warmth. You will have shed skins like a snake on fast-forward: the skin that believed love must hurt to be real, the skin that thought honesty was a weapon rather than a gift, the skin that mistook control for safety.  

I hope you keep one souvenir: a small matchbox containing the sound of Sorbia’s laugh the day she realized you mispronounced “isthmus” on purpose just to hear her correct you. Shake it whenever the afterlife grows too quiet.  

Remember the recipe. Remember the holes. Remember that distance is a teacher whose syllabus is written in disappearing ink.  

And if you ever pass through San Pedro Sula, look for a woman who paints her nails with White-Out. Tell her the comet finally came back. Tell her you learned to play the nocturne correctly, tempo intact. Tell her the raft held.  

With leftover avocado on my fingers,  
Armando

I squat, feel the chill through my jeans, and speak aloud the names of everyone I have failed to love well. The list is long; the weeds do not judge. Instead they offer modest miracles: a ladybug fastening its shell like a helmet, the tomato plant scenting the air with green optimism. I understand that the garden is the room’s antonym: where the room hoards, the garden relinquishes. Seeds abandon themselves to wind; weeds surrender their territory to frost.  

I tear out a page from the notebook I carry, tear the page into smaller pages, then into confetti. I bury the pieces among the roots. Tomorrow they will be unreadable, yesterday’s arrogance mulched into humus. Something else will grow here—maybe a phrase, maybe only the forgiveness of soil.  

I stand. The city’s lights flicker on, a constellation that refuses mythology. I think: perhaps the 2,500th word is not a milestone but an exit. I count the steps away from this paragraph and into whatever comes next.  

XVI. The unwritten novel  
There is a novel I will never write. Its protagonist is a woman who collects last words the way lepidopterists pin wings. She lives in a city built on top of another city; when the subway brakes, you can hear the dead one whisper. Every chapter begins with an epitaph she has stolen from a stranger’s deathbed. The plot refuses to cohere; it is a flock of birds that will not stay in formation. Critics would call it “fragmentary,” readers would call it “confusing,” my mother would call it “depressing.” So I keep it folded in a drawer, a paper kite that will never confront wind.  

Sometimes, late at night, I take the kite out and hold it up to the lamp. The light shines through the gaps where sentences should be. I pretend those gaps are windows, and that someone inside is looking back at me, equally afraid to finish what we started.  

XVII. Instructions for disappearing  
1. Begin by removing your name from the envelopes of bills. Let the electricity company guess who you were.  
2. Stop answering to your reflection. Nod politely in mirrors, then walk away mid-sentence.  
3. Plant something invasive—bamboo, mint, regret—so the garden argues your case after you’re gone.  
4. Leave the radio on, tuned to a station that plays only field recordings of airports.  
5. Forget your birthday on purpose. Feel the day pass like a stranger’s coat brushing your arm.  
6. Translate your diary into a language no one speaks, then lose the dictionary.  
7. Practice the smile that closes doors without sound.  
8. Finally, walk into the fog at the edge of town carrying nothing but the smell of rain on pavement. Do not look back; the town is learning to manage without your outlines.  

XVIII. The paradox of the second person  
I keep slipping into “you,” as if addressing someone outside the skin, when really the pronoun is a hand reaching back inside the ribcage. You, reader, are a conspiracy I invent to feel less alone. Yet the more I insist on you, the more I erase the border between us. Your pupils dilate; mine echo. Your heart makes a fist; mine rehearses the same shadow boxing. I write “you” and suddenly the room that is not a room contains two chairs, two cups of coffee cooling at different rates. The mirror finally consents to reflect, but the face is hybrid, half yours, half mine, like a photograph double-exposed by accident and declared art.  

XI. Recipe for something like happiness  
Take three ripe avocados, the kind that sigh when pressed.  
Add the juice of one lime that rolled under the fridge and gathered dust—its scars are flavor.  
Mince one small onion so fine that it dissolves into rumor.  
Salt until the mixture tastes like the first time you swore in front of your mother and she laughed instead of scolded.  
Fold in chopped cilantro, but only if your genes don’t make it taste like dish soap.  
Serve on toast burnt exactly to the color of your favorite childhood teddy bear after the dryer incident.  
Eat barefoot, standing at the kitchen counter, while the neighbor’s nocturne slips through the wall.  
Lick the bowl shamelessly; the bowl remembers every hunger you pretended not to have.  

XII. The paradox of distance  
The farther away someone is, the more permission we grant them to be perfect. A continent, an ocean, a silence of three unreturned messages—each mile polishes the statue we carve of the absent beloved. Up close, pores appear, contradictions, the way they chew too loudly. I wonder if God invented distance so we could practice forgiveness in gradients.  

XIII. Letter to my future ghost  
Dear Ghost of Me Who Has Finally Learned How to Leave,  
By the time you read this, the room that is not a room will have renovated itself again. Maybe the bulb has been replaced by something energy-efficient and smug. Maybe the chair with the short leg has been burned for warmth. You will have shed skins like a snake on fast-forward: the skin that believed love must hurt to be real, the skin that thought honesty was a weapon rather than a gift, the skin that mistook control for safety.  

I hope you keep one souvenir: a small matchbox containing the sound of Sorbia’s laugh the day she realized you mispronounced “isthmus” on purpose just to hear her correct you. Shake it whenever the afterlife grows too quiet.  

Remember the recipe. Remember the holes. Remember that distance is a teacher whose syllabus is written in disappearing ink.  

And if you ever pass through San Pedro Sula, look for a woman who paints her nails with White-Out. Tell her the comet finally came back. Tell her you learned to play the nocturne correctly, tempo intact. Tell her the raft held.  

With leftover avocado on my fingers,  
Armando

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
